name: Surgeon
summary: A physician who performs operative procedures to treat injury, disease, and deformity—evolving from barber-surgeons and battlefield operators to highly specialized hospital consultants.
duties: Assess patients, plan operations, perform surgery, manage postoperative care, and collaborate with anesthesia and nursing teams.
skills: Anatomy, dexterity, crisis decision-making, sterile technique, communication with patients and staff.
tools: Scalpels, clamps, sutures; modern laparoscopes, imaging guidance, robotic assistants.
regions: Global hospital systems; roots in Greco-Roman, Islamic, and early modern European medicine.
educationPath: Medical school, surgical residency, board certification and fellowships.
modernSectors: Hospitals, trauma centers, outpatient surgery, academic medicine.

events.label: Anesthesia in surgery
events.description: Ether and chloroform enable longer, more complex operations with patient unconsciousness.

events.label: Antiseptic surgery
events.description: Lister promotes carbolic acid methods, reducing postoperative infection deaths.

sources.title: History of surgery references
sources.note: Professional surgery distinct from barber-surgeons by 18th century in many cities.

name: Barber-Surgeon
summary: A dual trade practitioner who cut hair, pulled teeth, and performed minor surgery—common in medieval and early modern Europe before medicine professionalized.
duties: Bleed patients, amputate limbs, treat wounds, extract teeth, and provide grooming services in the same shop.
skills: Knife work, wound care, customer trade, basic anatomy from apprenticeship.
tools: Razors, bleeding bowls, saws, tooth keys, shop pole signage.
regions: Europe from Middle Ages through the 18th century.

events.label: London Company split
events.description: Barber-Surgeons Company separates barbers from surgeons in England.

name: Herbalist
summary: A healer who prepares plant-based remedies from roots, leaves, and barks—predating modern pharmacy and still practiced in traditional medicine systems.
duties: Identify plants, compound teas and tinctures, advise clients on dosage, and document seasonal harvesting.
skills: Botanical identification, formulation, listening, ethical sourcing.
tools: Mortar and pestle, drying racks, scales, reference herbals.
regions: China, India, Europe, Africa, and indigenous communities worldwide.

events.label: Printed herbals spread
events.description: Illustrated botanical manuals standardize recipes across regions.

name: Pharmacist
summary: A licensed professional who dispenses medicines, verifies prescriptions, and advises on drug safety—successor to the apothecary in regulated modern healthcare.
duties: Fill prescriptions, check interactions, counsel patients, manage inventory, and compound specialty doses.
skills: Pharmacology, attention to detail, regulatory compliance, patient communication.
tools: Pill counters, compounding lab equipment, pharmacy management software.
regions: Global; regulated practice in most industrialized health systems.
educationPath: Pharmacy doctorate or equivalent national licensure programs.
modernSectors: Retail pharmacies, hospital pharmacies, clinical research, mail-order fulfillment.

events.label: Synthetic drugs
events.description: Industrial pharmaceutical manufacturing shifts dispensing from hand-compounding to branded tablets.

name: Dentist
summary: A practitioner devoted to oral health who treats teeth and gums—split from barber-surgeons as dentistry became a distinct licensed field.
duties: Examine mouths, fill cavities, extract teeth, fit prosthetics, and educate patients on hygiene.
skills: Manual precision, radiograph reading, pain management, small-business management in private practice.
tools: Dental drill, mirrors, forceps, X-ray sensors, CAD/CAM crown mills.
regions: Urban professional practice worldwide since 18th-century specialization.
originLabel: Dedicated dental texts and teaching in France and beyond
educationPath: Dental school, licensure exams, continuing education.
modernSectors: Private clinics, hospital dentistry, public health programs, orthodontics.

events.label: Dental college founding
events.description: Formal schools emerge in Baltimore and spread professional standards.

name: Paramedic
summary: An emergency medical responder who provides advanced pre-hospital care—born from ambulance attendants and battlefield medicine into a credentialed profession.
duties: Stabilize patients at scenes, administer drugs and airway support, coordinate with hospitals, and document incidents.
skills: Triage, trauma protocols, calm leadership, radio communication, driving under pressure.
tools: Defibrillator, airway kits, intravenous supplies, ambulance stretcher systems.
regions: North America, Europe, Australia, and growing systems in Asia and Latin America.
educationPath: EMT progression, paramedic certification, recurrent skills testing.
modernSectors: Municipal EMS, private ambulance, event medical, air medical crews.

events.label: White Paper on trauma
events.description: US reports on accidental death spur modern EMS system design.

name: Veterinarian
summary: A medical professional who diagnoses and treats animals—from royal stable physicians in antiquity to modern small-animal and livestock practice.
duties: Examine animals, vaccinate herds, perform surgery, advise owners, and monitor food-supply health regulations.
skills: Comparative anatomy, epidemiology, client education, regulatory documentation.
tools: Stethoscope, vaccines, surgical packs, portable ultrasound, herd databases.
regions: Global; formal schools from 18th-century Europe.
educationPath: Veterinary medical degrees and national licensing.
modernSectors: Small-animal clinics, agriculture, public health inspection, research.

events.label: Bacteriology in animal disease
events.description: Germ theory improves livestock plague control and food safety.

name: Alchemist
summary: A practitioner who sought to transform matter and compound elixirs—blending proto-chemistry, metallurgy, and medicine in early modern workshops.
duties: Distill substances, assay ores, prepare tinctures, and document symbolic and practical experiments.
skills: Furnace operation, careful measurement, record keeping, patron negotiation.
tools: Alembics, crucibles, bellows, mortars, coded notebooks.
regions: Islamic world, Renaissance Europe, and court laboratories.
originLabel: Islamic and late medieval alchemical texts

events.label: Chemistry emerges
events.description: Boyle and successors recast practical chymistry apart from mystical transmutation goals.

name: Combat Medic
summary: A military health specialist who treats wounded soldiers under fire—descended from camp followers and surgeons' mates into trained corpsmen and medics.
duties: Apply tourniquets, manage hemorrhage, evacuate casualties, maintain aid station supplies, and train troops in first aid.
skills: Battlefield triage, trauma care, physical stamina, tactical communication.
tools: IFAK kits, litters, IV fluids, chest seals; historically surgeon's kit and wagons.
regions: Every major army; documented from ancient campaigns to modern conflicts.
educationPath: Military medical training pipelines, combat lifesaver courses, nursing or medic MOS.
modernSectors: Army, navy, air force medical corps, special operations support.

events.label: Ambulance corps formalized
events.description: Civil War-era systems accelerate organized battlefield evacuation.

name: Physiotherapist
summary: A rehabilitation specialist who restores movement after injury or illness—rooted in massage and orthopedic gymnastics, now a licensed allied health profession.
duties: Assess mobility, design exercise programs, apply manual therapy, and educate patients on recovery.
skills: Biomechanics, empathy, exercise prescription, documentation for insurers.
tools: Treatment tables, resistance bands, ultrasound units, gait analysis systems.
regions: Global hospitals and sports clinics since late 19th-century formalization.
modernSectors: Hospitals, sports teams, elder care, private clinics, home health.

events.label: Wartime rehabilitation
events.description: Mass casualties from World War I expand institutional rehab demand.

name: Mortician
summary: A funeral professional who prepares deceased persons for burial or cremation—combining embalming science, grief services, and logistical coordination.
duties: Embalm or prepare remains, arrange viewings, coordinate cemeteries, and support bereaved families.
skills: Cosmetic restoration, regulatory compliance, tact, business operations.
tools: Embalming machine, arterial tubes, caskets, hearses, refrigeration.
regions: Industrialized funeral industries; professionalized in 19th-century America and Europe.
originLabel: Civil War era embalming trade expansion
educationPath: Mortuary science programs, funeral director licensure, apprenticeship.
modernSectors: Funeral homes, crematories, pre-need planning firms.

events.label: Memorial broadcasting
events.description: Televised state funerals raise public awareness of funeral directing as a profession.

name: Radiographer
summary: A technologist who produces medical imaging with X-rays and related modalities—emerging alongside radiology after Röntgen's discovery.
duties: Position patients, operate imaging equipment, follow radiation safety protocols, and collaborate with radiologists.
skills: Anatomy positioning, equipment calibration, patient reassurance, PACS workflow.
tools: X-ray tubes, CT scanners, MRI consoles, lead shielding.
regions: Hospital systems worldwide since early 20th century.
educationPath: Radiography degrees, ARRT-style certification, continuing education.
modernSectors: Hospitals, outpatient imaging centers, mobile radiography, research.

events.label: CT scanning
events.description: Computed tomography adds cross-sectional imaging to technologist practice.

name: Soldier
summary: A member of an organized fighting force who carries out military orders in war and peacekeeping—the generic backbone role behind countless specialized branches.
duties: Train for combat, maintain equipment, follow command structure, patrol, and execute missions.
skills: Discipline, marksmanship, teamwork, endurance, rules of engagement literacy.
tools: Rifle, body armor, field pack, radios; historically spears, shields, muskets.
regions: Every state with standing or conscript armies.
educationPath: Basic training, specialty schools, NCO and officer pipelines.

events.label: Industrial total war
events.description: Mass conscription and trench systems redefine soldier experience in Europe.

name: Infantry
summary: Foot soldiers who form the core of most armies—fighting with small arms and close weapons from phalanxes to modern rifle squads.
duties: Advance on foot, hold ground, clear buildings, dig field fortifications, and coordinate with armor and air support.
skills: Marksmanship, small-unit tactics, land navigation, physical fitness.
tools: Rifles, machine guns, grenades, night vision; historically pikes and muskets.
regions: Universal across armies; dominant before mechanized cavalry returns in combined arms.
educationPath: Military basic training and infantry school courses.
modernSectors: Army infantry brigades, marine rifle companies, light infantry.

events.label: Mechanized combined arms
events.description: Tanks and radios integrate infantry into fast-moving battle groups.

name: Cavalryman
summary: A mounted soldier who fought on horseback with lance, saber, or carbine—decisive for millennia until mechanized armor and aircraft replaced horse mobility.
duties: Scout terrain, charge formations, pursue routed enemies, and escort officers and messengers.
skills: Horsemanship, mounted combat, reconnaissance, animal care.
tools: Saber, lance, carbine, saddle, remount lines.
regions: Eurasian steppes, European armies, and US frontier cavalry.

events.label: Last great cavalry charges
events.description: Machine guns and trenches end mass cavalry assaults on Western Front.

name: Musketeer
summary: An early modern infantry soldier armed with a smoothbore musket—firing in volleys before bayonet charges in European and colonial warfare.
duties: Load paper cartridges, maintain fire discipline, fix bayonets, and maneuver in line formations.
skills: Drill precision, tolerance for smoke and noise, bayonet fighting.
tools: Matchlock or flintlock musket, bayonet, cartridge box.
regions: Europe, Ottoman armies, and colonial campaigns 16th–19th centuries.

events.label: Bayonet standardization
events.description: Socket bayonets let musketeers fight as pseudo-pikemen without separate pike blocks.

name: Grenadier
summary: An elite infantry soldier originally trained to throw hand grenades over fortifications—later an honorific for tall, tough line companies.
duties: Assault breaches, throw grenades, lead shock attacks, and maintain elite drill standards.
skills: Courage under fire, accurate throwing, disciplined advance, intimidating presence.
tools: Hand grenades, musket, short sword, distinctive headgear historically.
regions: European armies especially 17th–19th centuries.

events.label: Elite status without grenades
events.description: Many armies keep grenadier titles while abandoning thrown grenades in open battle.

name: Sapper
summary: A combat engineer who breaches fortifications, clears mines, and builds field works—descended from miners and siege specialists in royal armies.
duties: Dig trenches, disarm explosives, build bridges under fire, and advise commanders on obstacle breaching.
skills: Explosives safety, field fortification, map reading, teamwork in high risk.
tools: Pickaxes, demolition charges, mine detectors, bulldozers in modern engineer units.
regions: Engineer corps in British, French, US, and many national armies.
modernSectors: Army engineers, EOD teams, bridge companies, NATO sapper schools.

events.label: Trench warfare
events.description: Western Front entrenched systems make sappers essential for underground mines and breaching.

name: Artillery Officer
summary: An officer who commands cannon crews and fire direction—evolving from gunner specialists into scientifically trained branches of modern armies.
duties: Calculate trajectories, coordinate batteries, maintain guns, and integrate fires with maneuver units.
skills: Ballistics math, leadership, map plotting, communications protocols.
tools: Range tables, fire direction centers, howitzers, radar; historically quadrant and sponge.
regions: Professional armies worldwide; royal artillery academies from 18th century.
educationPath: Military academy, artillery officer basic course, fire direction certification.
modernSectors: Army and marine artillery, air defense, naval gunnery coordination.

events.label: Barrage tactics
events.description: Industrial shell production makes artillery the dominant killer on Western Front.

name: Naval Officer
summary: A commissioned leader aboard warships who navigates, commands divisions, and executes fleet strategy—from age-of-sail lieutenants to modern nuclear sub captains.
duties: Navigate vessels, lead divisions, manage watches, interpret orders, and maintain discipline at sea.
skills: Seamanship, leadership, navigation, rules of engagement, engineering literacy on modern ships.
tools: Charts, sextants historically; radar, sonar, combat information centers today.
regions: Global naval powers; traditions in Britain, Spain, Netherlands, US, Japan.
educationPath: Naval academies, officer candidate schools, bridge watchstanding qualifications.
modernSectors: Surface fleet, submarines, naval aviation liaison, coast guard officer corps.

events.label: Nelson era fleet battles
events.description: Line-of-battle tactics peak before steam and ironclads transform naval command.

name: Privateer
summary: A privately owned ship authorized by a government to attack enemy commerce during war—blurring the line between sailor, merchant, and pirate.
duties: Cruise shipping lanes, capture prizes, bring vessels to admiralty courts, and avoid neutral violations.
skills: Navigation, gunnery, prize law literacy, crew management, risk appetite.
tools: Armed merchant hull, letters of marque, small arms, prize crews.
regions: Atlantic and Caribbean during European wars; American Revolution and War of 1812 peaks.
originLabel: Letters of marque in Atlantic trade wars

events.label: Paris Declaration
events.description: Many powers renounce privateering; standing navies replace commerce raiding licenses.

name: Mercenary
summary: A fighter paid for service outside patriotic conscription—ancient hoplite contractors to modern private military contractors advising and protecting clients.
duties: Execute contracts for security, training, or combat; maintain unit cohesion; operate under client rules.
skills: Weapons proficiency, languages, adaptability, contract discipline, risk assessment.
tools: Small arms, tactical vehicles, communications; historically condottieri retinues.
regions: Swiss pike blocks historically; global PMC presence in 20th–21st centuries.

events.label: Italian condottieri
events.description: City-states hire captain companies as semi-professional armies.

name: Drill Instructor
summary: A training non-commissioned officer who transforms recruits into disciplined soldiers through intensive drill—iconic in marine and army boot camps.
duties: Lead physical training, teach customs and courtesies, enforce standards, and evaluate readiness.
skills: Command voice, example-setting, stress management, instructional clarity.
tools: Whistle, training schedules, obstacle courses, marksmanship ranges.
regions: US marine corps and army traditions especially; parallels in many militaries.
educationPath: NCO promotion, drill instructor school, safety certifications.
modernSectors: Recruit depots, officer candidate support, reserve training commands.

events.label: WWII training expansion
events.description: Millions of conscripts pass through standardized basic training pipelines.

name: General
summary: A senior commander who plans campaigns and leads large formations—title and role dating to republican Rome and continuing in modern joint staffs.
duties: Set strategy, allocate forces, coordinate allies, and bear responsibility for operational outcomes.
skills: Strategic thinking, staff leadership, logistics grasp, political communication.
tools: Maps, staff sections, secure communications, intelligence fusion cells.
regions: Staff commands in national capitals and theater headquarters worldwide.
educationPath: Military academy, war college, joint professional military education.
modernSectors: Army, joint chiefs structures, coalition commands, peacekeeping HQs.

events.label: Combined operations
events.description: D-Day scale invasions require generals to integrate air, sea, and land components.

name: Squire
summary: A young attendant who served a knight by caring for armor and horses while training for knighthood—a medieval apprenticeship on the path to elite warrior status.
duties: Maintain arms, accompany lords, train in horsemanship and combat, and manage camp logistics.
skills: Horsemanship, armor care, courtesy, hunting, basic arms practice.
tools: Horse tack, polishing kit, practice sword, shield.
regions: Medieval and early modern European feudal societies.

events.label: Nobility transforms
events.description: Standing armies and gunpowder reduce the knight-squire pathway in many states.

name: Samurai
summary: A warrior-retainer class in feudal Japan who served daimyo with swordsmanship, administration, and cultural refinement—legally abolished in the Meiji modernization.
duties: Fight in clan armies, govern estates, advise lords, and embody bushido ideals in peace and war.
skills: Kenjutsu, archery on horseback, literacy, loyalty politics, ritual decorum.
tools: Katana, wakizashi, yumi bow, armor (yoroi), war banners.
regions: Japan, especially Edo period domains until 1868.
originLabel: Rise of bushi power through Kamakura and Edo eras

events.label: Tokugawa peace
events.description: Centuries of relative internal peace shift many samurai toward administration and arts.

events.label: Meiji abolition
events.description: Hairstyle and sword privileges end; western-style conscript army replaces samurai class.
